Tips: Eye contact, firm handshake, sit up straight, speak clearly, say 'Thank you' at the end.
spoken-english-30day.example:
Mock interview for a school prefect position:

Interviewer: Tell me about yourself.
Candidate: My name is Arjun and I'm in Class 10. I'm passionate about cricket and I've been the team captain for two years. I also volunteer at the school library every Wednesday.

Interviewer: Why do you want to be a school prefect?
Candidate: I believe I can help bridge the gap between students and teachers. I'm approachable, organised, and I genuinely care about making our school better.

Interviewer: What's your biggest weakness?
Candidate: I sometimes take on too many things at once. But I've been learning to prioritise and say no when my plate is full.
spoken-english-30day.activity:
Practise answering these 3 interview questions aloud:
1. 'Tell me about yourself.' (60 seconds max)
2. 'What's your greatest strength?' (with an example)
3. 'Why should we choose you?'

Record yourself and listen back. Do you sound confident and clear?

vocab.example_sentence: My greatest strength is my ability to work in a team.
My weakness is that I sometimes overthink things.
I have experience leading the school debate team.
This interview is a great opportunity for me.
I believe I'm qualified for this role.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Tell me about yourself.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I'm [name], a Class 10 student. I love English and cricket, and I lead our school's quiz club.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: What's your greatest strength?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I'm a good communicator. For example, I represented our school in an inter-school debate last year.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Why should we select you?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Because I'm dedicated, responsible, and I truly want to make a positive difference.

spoken-english-30day.speaking_task: Do a full mock interview with the AI. Answer at least 4 interview questions. The AI plays a friendly interviewer and gives feedback after each answer.
spoken-english-30day.bengali_tip: Bengali speakers often answer 'Tell me about yourself' with their full family history: 'My father is a government employee, my mother is a homemaker...' — In English interviews, focus on YOU: your skills, interests, and achievements.
spoken-english-30day.micro_grammar: 'I believe I can...' / 'I'm passionate about...' / 'I have experience in...' — These phrases show confidence without being arrogant. They're interview gold.

questions.question: When asked 'Tell me about yourself' in an interview, you should focus on:
questions.explanation: Interviews are about YOU — your skills, interests, and relevant achievements. Keep it focused and professional.

questions.question: You should always ask at least one question at the end of an interview.
questions.explanation: Yes! Asking a question shows you're interested and engaged. 'What would a typical day look like?' is a good one.

questions.question: Complete: 'My greatest ___ is my ability to communicate well.'
questions.explanation: 'My greatest strength' is the standard phrase for describing your best quality in an interview.

safeFeedback.pass: Interview-ready! You'll impress anyone with these skills.
safeFeedback.fail: Review interview tips and practise your answers aloud!

spoken-english-30day.title: Confidence Hacks
spoken-english-30day.explanation:
The biggest barrier to speaking English isn't grammar — it's fear. Today we tackle confidence directly.

10 Confidence Hacks for Speaking English:
1. Speak slowly — fast speech signals nervousness.
2. Use filler phrases: 'Well...', 'Let me think...', 'That's a great question.'
3. Don't apologise for your English — own it.
4. Accept mistakes — even native speakers make them.
5. Practise in front of a mirror daily — 5 minutes is enough.
6. Think in English, not Bengali → translate → English.
7. Record yourself and listen — you'll improve fast.
8. Use simple sentences — complex ≠ better.
9. Read aloud for 10 minutes daily.
10. Celebrate small wins — every conversation in English is progress!
spoken-english-30day.example:
Before and After confidence:

BEFORE (nervous):
'Uh... sorry, my English is not so good... I am trying to say that... um... I think... sorry...'

AFTER (confident):
'That's a great question. Let me think for a moment... I believe that online learning has both advantages and disadvantages. On one hand, it's flexible. On the other hand, it can be isolating. Overall, I think a blend of both works best.'

The difference? Not grammar — confidence, pacing, and filler phrases!
spoken-english-30day.activity:
Do the 'Mirror Challenge':
1. Stand in front of a mirror.
2. Introduce yourself confidently.
3. Give a 60-second opinion on any topic.
4. Use at least 3 filler phrases naturally.
5. Do NOT apologise for your English even once.

Repeat this every morning for the rest of the course!

vocab.example_sentence: Don't hesitate — just start talking!
Every attempt at speaking English makes you better.
Mistakes are proof that you're trying.
You've improved so much in just 24 days!
Fluency comes from practice, not perfection.

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: I'm too scared to speak English in front of others.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Don't worry! Everyone makes mistakes. The key is to keep trying.

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: But what if people laugh?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: People who laugh aren't worth worrying about. Most people appreciate the effort.

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: What if I forget a word mid-sentence?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Just say 'let me think' or describe the word differently. That's what confident speakers do!

spoken-english-30day.speaking_task: Talk to the AI for 2 minutes about any topic without stopping or apologising for your English. Use filler phrases like 'Well...', 'That's interesting...', 'Let me think...' to keep going.
spoken-english-30day.bengali_tip: The #1 Bengali speaker habit to break: saying 'Sorry, my English is not good' before speaking. NEVER apologise for your English! Just speak. Confidence = 80% of fluency.
spoken-english-30day.micro_grammar: Filler phrases (not grammar, but essential for fluency): 'Well...' | 'You know...' | 'Let me think...' | 'That's a great question.' | 'How do I put this...' These buy you time and make you sound natural.

questions.question: What should you do when you forget a word while speaking?
options: Use a filler phrase and describe the word.
questions.explanation: Use a filler like 'Let me think...' and then describe the word or use a synonym. This is what confident speakers do!

questions.question: Saying 'Sorry, my English is not good' before speaking shows politeness.
questions.explanation: It's not politeness — it's self-sabotage! Never apologise for your English. Just speak confidently.

questions.question: What's a good filler phrase to use when you need time to think?
questions.explanation: 'Let me think...' buys you time naturally. Other options: 'Well...' or 'That's a great question.'

safeFeedback.pass: Your confidence toolkit is ready! Use it every day.
safeFeedback.fail: Remember: confidence > grammar. Review the hacks and try again!

spoken-english-30day.title: Slang & Natural English
spoken-english-30day.explanation:
Textbook English is important, but real conversational English includes slang and informal expressions. Knowing these makes you sound natural.

Common informal English:
- 'gonna' = going to ('I'm gonna study tonight.')
- 'wanna' = want to ('Do you wanna grab lunch?')
- 'kinda' = kind of ('I'm kinda tired.')
- 'awesome' = great ('That's awesome!')
- 'no worries' = it's fine ('No worries about the delay.')
- 'hang on' = wait a moment ('Hang on, let me check.')
- 'my bad' = my mistake ('Oh, my bad!')
- 'chill' = relax ('Just chill, it'll be fine.')

IMPORTANT: Only use slang in casual settings — friends, social media, texts. Never in exams, interviews, or formal letters!
spoken-english-30day.example:
Two friends chatting after school:

Kabir: Hey! Wanna grab some phuchka on the way home?
Riya: Definitely! I'm kinda starving right now.
Kabir: Awesome! There's a new stall near the metro. Everyone says it's amazing.
Riya: Sounds good! Oh wait — I forgot my wallet at school. My bad!
Kabir: No worries! I'll treat you today.
Riya: You're the best! I'll pay you back tomorrow.
Kabir: Chill, it's just phuchka. Let's go!
spoken-english-30day.activity:
Rewrite these formal sentences in casual/slang English:
1. 'I am going to visit my grandmother this weekend.' → 
2. 'Do you want to come to my birthday party?' → 
3. 'That is wonderful news!' → 
4. 'I made a mistake. I apologise.' → 
5. 'Please wait a moment.' → 

Say both versions aloud — feel the difference in tone!

vocab.example_sentence: I'm gonna ace this test!
Do you wanna watch a movie tonight?
That goal was absolutely awesome!
Just chill — everything will work out.
Oh, my bad! I sent the wrong file.

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: Hey! Wanna hang out after school?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Yeah, sounds awesome! Where do you wanna go?

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: I'm kinda craving momos. There's a new place near the park.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Let's do it! Oh wait — I'm gonna be late if I don't finish this homework.

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: No worries! Just meet me there in an hour.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Cool, see you then!

spoken-english-30day.speaking_task: Have a fully casual conversation with the AI as if you're texting a friend in person. Use at least 5 slang words: gonna, wanna, kinda, awesome, chill, my bad, no worries.
spoken-english-30day.bengali_tip: Bengali teens often use 'too good' as slang for 'awesome'. While Indians understand it, international speakers won't. Use 'awesome', 'amazing', or 'incredible' instead — they're universally understood.
spoken-english-30day.micro_grammar: Formal vs Casual register: 'I would like to go' (formal) → 'I wanna go' (casual). 'I am going to study' (formal) → 'I'm gonna study' (casual). NEVER use slang in exams or formal writing!

questions.question: What does 'gonna' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Gonna' is the casual/slang form of 'going to'. 'I'm gonna study' = 'I'm going to study'.

questions.question: It's appropriate to use 'wanna' and 'gonna' in a school exam.
questions.explanation: Never! Slang is for casual speech and texting only. Always use formal English in exams and official writing.

questions.question: What casual phrase means 'my mistake'?
questions.explanation: 'My bad' is a casual, friendly way to acknowledge a mistake in informal conversations.

safeFeedback.pass: You now sound like a natural English speaker! Know when to be formal and when to be casual.
safeFeedback.fail: Review slang vs formal — know when to use each!

spoken-english-30day.title: Think in English
spoken-english-30day.explanation:
The ultimate fluency hack: THINK in English. Right now, you probably think in Bengali, translate to English, then speak. This creates a delay. The goal is to skip the translation step entirely.

How to start thinking in English:
1. Narrate your day mentally: 'I'm walking to the bus stop. The weather is nice today.'
2. Label objects: 'That's a chair. That's a window. That car is red.'
3. Think about your opinions: 'I think this movie is boring.'
4. Plan in English: 'After school, I'm gonna study maths, then play cricket.'
5. Dream in English: When your dreams switch to English, you've made it!

Start with 5 minutes of English thinking daily. Increase to 30 minutes over time.
spoken-english-30day.example:
Internal monologue practice — think these thoughts:

'OK, it's Monday morning. I need to get ready for school. Where's my uniform? There it is. I should hurry up because the bus comes at 7:30. What do I want for breakfast? I think I'll have toast and tea today. Oh no, I forgot to finish my science homework! I'll do it during the break. Right, let me pack my bag — books, notebook, water bottle, pencil case. Ready! Let's go.'

This whole paragraph happened IN your head, in English — no Bengali needed!
spoken-english-30day.activity:
The 5-Minute English Brain Challenge:

For the next 5 minutes, think ONLY in English. No Bengali in your head.

1. Look around your room — describe 5 things in English.
2. Plan your evening — in English.
3. Think about your favourite movie — why do you like it? (in English)

If you catch yourself thinking in Bengali, gently switch back. It gets easier with practice!

vocab.example_sentence: Stop translating from Bengali — think directly in English!
Thinking in English is the fastest path to fluency.
With practice, English becomes an instinct, not a task.
Your English responses will become automatic over time.
Make thinking in English a daily habit.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: What's the biggest challenge in speaking English fluently?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I think the biggest challenge is that I always translate from Bengali first.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: That's very common. How can you fix it?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: By thinking in English! I've started narrating my day in my head in English.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: That's excellent! How is it going?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: It's getting easier. I still switch to Bengali sometimes, but I catch myself now.

spoken-english-30day.speaking_task: Do a 3-minute stream of consciousness with the AI — describe what you see around you, your plans for the day, and your feelings right now. The goal: zero translation from Bengali. Just think and speak in English!
spoken-english-30day.bengali_tip: The Bengali→English translation habit is your biggest speed bump. Start replacing it: instead of thinking 'আমি ক্ষুধার্ত' → 'I am hungry', train yourself to think 'I'm hungry' directly. Your brain can learn this in 2–3 weeks of daily practice.
spoken-english-30day.micro_grammar: Internal monologue grammar: Use present continuous for what's happening now ('I'm sitting at my desk'), simple present for facts ('My room has a window'), and 'going to/gonna' for plans ('I'm gonna study after lunch').

questions.question: What is the fastest way to improve English fluency?
options: Think in English instead of translating.
questions.explanation: Thinking in English eliminates the translation delay. This is the single most effective fluency hack.

questions.question: You should narrate your daily activities in English in your head to build fluency.
questions.explanation: Yes! Mental narration in English builds the habit of thinking directly in the language.

questions.question: What process should you SKIP for faster English speaking? (Bengali → ___ → English)
questions.explanation: The translation step from Bengali to English slows you down. Think directly in English to skip it.

safeFeedback.pass: You understand the key to fluency! Now practise it every day.
safeFeedback.fail: Review the tips for thinking in English — then try again!

spoken-english-30day.title: Real-Life Simulation
spoken-english-30day.explanation:
Today you simulate 5 real-life situations in English, one after another. This is your dress rehearsal before the final test.

The 5 scenarios:
1. At a restaurant — order a meal.
2. At a shop — buy a gift for a friend.
3. Meeting someone new — introduce yourself and find common ground.
4. A phone call — call a friend to plan an outing.
5. A mini speech — speak for 60 seconds on 'What I learned this month.'

The goal: move through all 5 without breaking into Bengali. Use everything you've learned — vocab, grammar, confidence hacks, and fillers!
spoken-english-30day.example:
Scenario 3 — Meeting someone new:

You: Hi! I'm [name]. I don't think we've met. Are you also here for the quiz competition?
New person: Yes! I'm from a school in Howrah. I'm kind of nervous.
You: No worries — same here! What topics have you prepared?
New person: Mostly science and current affairs. You?
You: History and English. We should combine our strengths!
New person: That's a great idea! We have a lot in common.
You: Definitely! Let's exchange numbers and practice together.
spoken-english-30day.activity:
Run through all 5 scenarios yourself — speak aloud for each one:

1. Restaurant: Order food, ask for a recommendation, request the bill.
2. Shopping: Ask about a product, negotiate price, decide to buy.
3. New person: Introduce yourself, find 2 shared interests.
4. Phone call: Plan a meeting — time, place, what to bring.
5. Mini speech: 60 seconds on your favourite topic.

Time yourself — can you do all 5 in under 10 minutes?

vocab.meaning: অনুকরণ করা / নকল পরিস্থিতি তৈরি করা
vocab.example_sentence: Let's simulate a real conversation at a restaurant.
In this scenario, you are buying a birthday gift.
Try to sound natural, not rehearsed.
Good speakers adapt to different situations.
This simulation is your dress rehearsal for real life.

dialogue.speaker: AI Waiter
dialogue.line: Welcome! Table for one?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Yes please. Could I see the menu?

dialogue.speaker: AI Waiter
dialogue.line: Here you go. I'd recommend the paneer tikka today.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: That sounds great! I'd like that and a sweet lassi, please.

dialogue.speaker: AI Waiter
dialogue.line: Excellent choice! Anything else?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: No, that's all. Could we have the bill when I'm done? Thank you!

spoken-english-30day.speaking_task: Run through all 5 real-life scenarios with the AI playing different roles: waiter, shopkeeper, new friend, phone friend, and audience. Switch between them naturally. This is your ultimate practice round!
spoken-english-30day.bengali_tip: In real-life situations, don't wait for the 'perfect' sentence in your head. Just START speaking. You can fix and refine as you go. Imperfect English that flows is always better than perfect English that never comes out.
spoken-english-30day.micro_grammar: Polite requests recap: 'Could I...' (restaurants, shops) | 'Would you mind...' (asking favours) | 'I'd like...' (ordering) | 'Do you think you could...' (big requests). Master these 4 and you can handle any situation.

questions.question: Which is the best approach when you can't find the right word?
options: Stop speaking and think for a long time.
Describe the word differently and keep going.
questions.explanation: Describing the word or using a synonym keeps the conversation flowing. This is what confident speakers do!

questions.question: Imperfect English that flows is better than perfect English that never comes out.
questions.explanation: Absolutely! Communication is the goal. You can always improve grammar later — but you can't communicate if you don't speak.

questions.question: What polite phrase starts a request at a restaurant? '___ I see the menu, please?'
questions.explanation: 'Could I see the menu, please?' is the standard polite request at a restaurant.

safeFeedback.pass: You handled all scenarios like a pro! Ready for the final test.
safeFeedback.fail: Review the simulation scenarios and try once more!

spoken-english-30day.title: Final Test
spoken-english-30day.explanation:
This is your FINAL TEST — covering everything from all 4 weeks!

✅ Week 1: Introductions, school, hobbies, routines, questions, conversations
✅ Week 2: Food ordering, shopping, directions, phone calls, social media, opinions
✅ Week 3: Strangers, friends, group discussions, storytelling, debate, emotions
✅ Week 4: Public speaking, interviews, confidence, slang, thinking in English, simulations

5 questions covering all 4 weeks. You need 70% to pass. Take a deep breath — you've worked hard for this!
spoken-english-30day.example:
Final review power phrases:

🗣️ 'I'm [name]. Nice to meet you!'
🍽️ 'I'd like a [dish], please.'
🗺️ 'Could you tell me how to get to...?'
📞 'This is [name]. Could I speak to...?'
💬 'In my opinion...' / 'I strongly believe...'
📖 'Last year, something interesting happened...'
🎤 'I'd like to leave you with this thought...'
😊 'I'm thrilled!' / 'I feel grateful because...'
spoken-english-30day.activity:
The Ultimate 5-Minute Challenge before your test:

1. Introduce yourself (15 seconds)
2. Order food politely (15 seconds)
3. Give directions to your school (15 seconds)
4. Share an opinion with a reason (30 seconds)
5. Tell a short story (60 seconds)
6. Express how you feel about finishing this course (30 seconds)

Do it all in one go — no stopping!

vocab.example_sentence: You've accomplished something incredible in 30 days!
Your fluency has improved dramatically.
This final test is a major milestone.
These 30 days have transformed your English confidence.
This is just the beginning of your English journey.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: How do you feel before the final test?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I'm a bit nervous, but mostly excited. I've learned so much!

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: What's the most important thing you learned?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: That confidence matters more than perfect grammar.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Absolutely! Are you ready?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Yes! Let me think... Actually, I'm ready. Let's do this!

spoken-english-30day.speaking_task: Give a 2-minute speech to the AI about your 30-day English journey. Cover: what you learned, your favourite lesson, your biggest improvement, and your plans to keep practising.
spoken-english-30day.bengali_tip: You've come from 'My name is being...' to confidently expressing opinions, debating, and telling stories. The journey doesn't end here — keep thinking in English, keep speaking, keep making beautiful mistakes!
spoken-english-30day.micro_grammar: Full course grammar recap: Simple Present (routines) | Past Simple (stories) | 'I'd like' (polite requests) | Question structure (WH + helping verb) | 'I believe/think' (opinions) | Fillers (Well, Let me think) | Contractions (I'm, gonna, wanna)

questions.question: Which introduction is the most natural?
options: I'm Arjun. Nice to meet you!
questions.explanation: 'I'm Arjun. Nice to meet you!' is natural, confident, and exactly how native speakers introduce themselves.

questions.question: What should you do if you forget a word mid-conversation?
options: Use a filler and describe the word.
questions.explanation: Use a filler ('Let me think...') and describe the word differently. This keeps the conversation flowing naturally.

questions.question: 'Yesterday I went to the market' correctly uses past tense.
questions.explanation: Yes! 'Went' is the past tense of 'go'. This is a correct past tense sentence.

questions.question: Which phrase is used to END a speech powerfully?
options: I'd like to leave you with this thought...
questions.explanation: 'I'd like to leave you with this thought...' is a powerful, professional way to conclude any speech.

questions.question: What's the #1 rule for English fluency?
questions.explanation: Thinking in English — without translating from Bengali — is the single most effective path to fluency.

safeFeedback.pass: 🎉🏆 CONGRATULATIONS! You've completed the 30-Day Spoken English Course! You're now an English speaker — not a learner. Keep going!
safeFeedback.fail: You're so close! Review your weakest areas and try the final test one more time. You can do this!

spoken-english-30day.title: Speaking Challenge — Day 1
spoken-english-30day.explanation:
Welcome to the Speaking Challenge! These last 2 days are all about using your English in extended, creative ways.

Challenge Day 1 has 3 tasks:

Task 1 — 2-Minute Self Introduction: Not just name and school. Talk about your dreams, values, what makes you unique, and what you're passionate about.

Task 2 — Teach Something: Explain how to do something (cook a dish, play a game, solve a maths problem) in English. Teaching is the highest form of understanding.

Task 3 — Debate Yourself: Pick a topic and argue BOTH sides for 60 seconds each. This proves you can think flexibly in English.
spoken-english-30day.example:
Task 2 example — Teaching how to make chai:

'OK, so I'm going to teach you how to make the perfect chai! First, take a small pot and add one cup of water. Then add half a cup of milk. Put it on medium heat. While it's heating, add one teaspoon of sugar, a small piece of ginger, and two cardamom pods. Let it come to a boil. Then add one teaspoon of tea leaves. Let it boil for about 2 minutes until the colour turns dark. Finally, strain it into a cup and enjoy! Pro tip: crushing the cardamom releases more flavour.'
spoken-english-30day.activity:
Complete all 3 challenges:

1. Record a 2-minute self introduction that goes beyond basic facts.
2. Teach the AI how to do something you're good at — step by step.
3. Debate yourself on: 'Is social media making us less social?' — argue both sides.

Each challenge should be spoken without reading from notes!

vocab.example_sentence: This speaking challenge will test everything you've learned.
My passion is playing music and teaching others.
What makes you unique is your combination of skills.
Good debaters have flexible thinking.
Can you explain how to solve this problem?

dialogue.speaker: AI
dialogue.line: For your first challenge, give a 2-minute introduction that goes beyond basics.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I'm [name] and I want to tell you what drives me. I'm passionate about...

dialogue.speaker: AI
dialogue.line: Great start! Now teach me something you're good at.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Sure! I'll teach you how to play cricket. First, you need a bat and a ball...

dialogue.speaker: AI
dialogue.line: Excellent teaching! Now debate yourself — social media: good or bad?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: For: Social media connects people globally. Against: But it reduces real conversations.

spoken-english-30day.speaking_task: Complete all 3 challenges with the AI: deep self-intro, teach something step-by-step, and debate both sides of a topic. The AI will give you feedback after each one.
spoken-english-30day.bengali_tip: In the self-introduction challenge, don't just list facts. Tell a STORY about yourself. 'I discovered my love for English when I first watched an English movie without subtitles and understood it!' — stories are memorable.
spoken-english-30day.micro_grammar: Sequencing for teaching: 'First... Then... After that... Next... Finally...' These words create clear, easy-to-follow instructions. Always use them when explaining a process.

questions.question: What makes a self-introduction memorable?
options: Listing your marks in every subject.
questions.explanation: Stories make you memorable. Share what drives you, a defining moment, or what makes you unique.

questions.question: When teaching something, you should use sequence words like 'first', 'then', 'finally'.
questions.explanation: Correct! Sequence words make your instructions clear and easy to follow.

questions.question: What skill does debating BOTH sides of a topic show?
questions.explanation: Arguing both sides shows flexible thinking — the ability to understand and articulate multiple perspectives.

safeFeedback.pass: Amazing performance! One more day to go!
safeFeedback.fail: Try the challenges again — focus on structure and natural flow!

spoken-english-30day.title: Speaking Challenge — Day 2 (Graduation!)
spoken-english-30day.explanation:
🎓 THIS IS YOUR GRADUATION DAY!

You started 30 days ago barely able to introduce yourself. Now you can order food, debate topics, tell stories, express emotions, give speeches, and think in English.

Final Challenge — The 5-Minute English Marathon:
Speak for 5 continuous minutes on the topic: 'My 30-Day English Journey — What I Learned, How I Changed, and Where I'm Going.'

Include:
- Your starting point (how was your English on Day 1?)
- Your favourite lesson and why
- The hardest challenge you overcame
- How your confidence has changed
- Your plan to keep improving

This is YOUR moment. Own it. Be proud. You earned this.
spoken-english-30day.example:
Sample graduation speech opening:

'Thirty days ago, I could barely introduce myself in English without fumbling. Today, I'm standing here — well, sitting here — giving a full speech in English about my journey. And honestly? I'm thrilled about how far I've come.

The lesson that changed everything for me was Day 24 — Confidence Hacks. I realised that perfect grammar doesn't matter as much as just speaking. My favourite moment was when I successfully ordered food in English at a restaurant and the waiter didn't even realize I was nervous!

Going forward, I plan to think in English for 15 minutes every day, read one English article before bed, and practise speaking with my friends. This is not the end — it's the beginning.'
spoken-english-30day.activity:
THE FINAL CHALLENGE:

Speak for 5 continuous minutes about your English learning journey.

Record yourself. This is your graduation recording — something you can listen to in 6 months and be proud of.

After recording, listen back and note:
- How natural do you sound?
- Did you use fillers well?
- Did you express emotions?
- Are you proud? (You should be!)

🎓 Congratulations, graduate!

vocab.example_sentence: Today I graduate from the 30-day English course!
This 30-day journey has transformed my confidence.
I'm so proud of how far I've come.
Let's reflect on what we've learned this month.
This is not the end — it's just the beginning!

dialogue.speaker: AI
dialogue.line: Congratulations on reaching Day 30! How do you feel?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I'm absolutely thrilled and proud. I can't believe how far I've come!

dialogue.speaker: AI
dialogue.line: What was the most valuable lesson?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Learning to think in English. It changed everything for me.

dialogue.speaker: AI
dialogue.line: What's your plan going forward?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I'm going to keep practising every day. This is just the beginning of my English journey!

spoken-english-30day.speaking_task: Deliver your 5-minute graduation speech to the AI. Cover: your starting point, best lesson, biggest challenge, how you've changed, and your future plans. The AI will celebrate your achievement and give final feedback!
spoken-english-30day.bengali_tip: Your final Bengali tip: You are now an English SPEAKER, not a learner. The difference? Speakers make mistakes and keep going. Learners wait for perfection. Be a speaker. Always. 🎓
spoken-english-30day.micro_grammar: Your complete grammar toolkit: Present Simple (habits) | Past Simple (stories) | 'I'd like' (requests) | Questions (WH + do/does) | Opinions ('I believe') | Fillers ('Well...') | Connectors ('However', 'Therefore') | Slang ('gonna', 'awesome'). You have everything you need!

questions.question: What's the difference between an English 'speaker' and a 'learner'?
options: Speakers make mistakes and keep going.
questions.explanation: Speakers make mistakes and keep going — that's the key difference. Learners wait for perfection and never start.

questions.question: After this 30-day course, you should stop practising English.
questions.explanation: Never stop! This course gave you the foundation — now keep building on it every day.

questions.question: Complete this graduation thought: 'This is not the end — it's just the ___.'
questions.explanation: 'This is just the beginning' — your English journey continues beyond these 30 days!

hints: Every ending is a new beginning

safeFeedback.pass: 🎓🏆🎉 YOU DID IT! 30 days complete. You are officially a confident English speaker. The world is yours — go speak it!
safeFeedback.fail: You're at the finish line! Review and try once more — you deserve that graduation badge!
